title: 2025/10/08 - GA Subcommittee A (2 of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-10-08 - Governance and Administration Committee - Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill [Subcommittee A]
description: Wednesday, 8th October 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: Zero Waste Aotearoa Sue Coutts Environment Southland Nichol Howell, Chairman Wellington City Council Environmental Mayoral Forum Piriakau Tribal Authority - Incorporated Jason Ake
descriptionSummary: Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill. Zero Waste Aotearoa. Sue Coutts

chunks.text: If you have zero waste out here online, please uh, join us and turn on your video. Hello. Welcome. Hey everyone. Welcome to the Select Committee. I hope you're having a good day. Yes, and you have 10 minutes with us and the time starts now. Oh, thanks so much for the opportunity to talk with you about the changes proposed in this local government systems improvement.

Bill. I'm really glad to hear that you had a short break before I came on. So, um, yeah, that's good to hear. You guys do get some time in your day to breathe. Uh, at first glance, you might think Zero Waste Style Tower Row is an environmental organization. Uh, we're also business people. We have 72 full members across the country that run businesses that provide reuse, recycling, composting and education services in their local communities.

And across our network, we provide 1100 jobs, um, meaningful secure jobs. We recycle and compost 38,000 tons every year. We turn over about 80 million, which supports our local economies. Our members include organizations like Ulta Zero Waste up in Kadi, who focus on reuse and recycling of construction and demolition waste.

Oranga Zero Waste is the first Maori Pacific led reuse and recycling center up in South Auckland and Tamaki and Wide Roll Zero Waste Supply reusable goods to hundreds of community organizations across Auckland. And that includes a lot of different ethnic and migrant organizations up there. Uh, I got my start in this sector running Wastes and Monica.

Uh, we all care about this bill because our work across the motto is all about increasing wellbeing of our people and our places. Uh, we, um, we work at the heart of our communities running businesses and organizations that create good, secure jobs for local people. We provide services that help households, businesses, and visitors to prevent and reduce waste.

And that has a really practical impact on community development, our local economies, and it helps us to protect our and restore our environment. So as you would expect, we don't support removing the four wellbeings from the local government acts. I'm sure you've had a lot of chat about that already today. Uh, what I really wanna focus on is the implications of including waste management in the list of core activities.

Um, on the surface of it, the change looks pretty straightforward, but it's actually part of a radical shift away from proactive investment by both central and local government and waste minimization infrastructure and services. The bill would add waste management as a core activity, but wouldn't include waste minimization. Um, the bill defines waste management as solid waste collection and disposal, and that only covers off rubbish.

This cha, allowing this change would limit the ability of councils and communities to invest in alternatives to disposal. And that's things like reuse, um, recycling, composting, um, education and behavior change programs. And it will lock councils and communities into a situation where they face steadily increasing costs to collect and dispose of increasing volumes of solar waste over time.

But it, and which is great if you're in the rubbish collection and disposal business, but it's not so great if you're in, if you wanna prevent and reduce waste and give households and businesses and visitors practical options for reuse, recycling and composting. When we looked into this, we spent quite a bit of time on it. We couldn't find any explanation of the reasoning behind this change.

Um, the only reference to it was a cabinet paper footnote that said, cabinet agreed that waste management and minimization facilities infrastructure and waste management of core services, but waste minimization services are not. And that's not consistent in the, um, wording that's proposed for the bill. Um, or the, um, current waste minimization acts creating like false separation like that will really restrict how councils can fund the activities in their WM MMPs and it would make it really hard for them to allocate their waste management

and minimize the budget minimization budgets in the way that they intend. Um, a focus on managing waste means communities will face steadily increasing costs and liabilities over time. And this is contrary to the bill's intent to use rate payer, um, funds efficiently. Embedding waste management in this way will take us down the wrong path.

The government's intent to spend to limit council spending on certain activities using rates, pegs, or caps will really limit spending on anything not considered core business. Um, if waste minimization is excluded from this list of core services, it's going to be really hard for councils to justify spending to prevent, reduce and minimize waste.

There's already been so many radical changes made in the last 18 months to how waste minimization is funded, and we've seen the, um, the way, um, central government spends its share of the waste disposal levy means that changes to the act last year mean that only a very small proportion of the waste disposal fund waste disposal levy funds that are collected, um, and given to central government, are now being allocated to minimizing waste.

A large proportion of that money is being spent on cleanups and other environmental activities, and this has a really perverse outcome in that it makes governments spending on these environmental programs totally dependent on high volume of rubbish continuing to go into landfill so that those waste disposal funds keep flowing in.

And the proposed changes to the way local government spends its share of waste levy, uh, signaled in the Waste Minimization Act amendment consultation, we'd see local government ability to spend on, um, waste minimization, uh, expanded out cleanups and other environmental activities too. And that means that council's waste minimization fund allocations wouldn't be ring-fenced anymore, which will make it really hard to justify spending on, um, waste minimization and reduction when you're, um, doing battle around the council table for priorities.

So, um, we know that waste minimization reduces risk, cost and liabilities, which is a critical thing that we want to see. Um, you know, coming through with this, the intent of this bill in 2006, the officer of the audit auditor General looked into, um, council waste management planning. They found that most of their activities involve managing waste that already existed.

Um, rather than reducing the quantity of waste generated over time, they said, if we don't reduce the amount of waste generated, councils will expect to manage steady or increasing quantities of waste. And that puts increasing demands on systems and budgets over time. And what we can draw from that is that councils will be locking themselves and their rate payers into an unsustainable and unaffordable trajectory.

Um, that's what we see this, um, change to the build doing. Um, reducing demand for infrastructure and services is critical. Um, waste minimization should remain a priority for councils so they can continue to invest in quality infrastructure and services that'll reduce long-term costs, risks, and liabilities for their communities.

Preventing and redu and, and minimizing waste, reduces demand for infrastructure and services. That means that communities and councils don't have to spend so much on these in the future. And this is in line with the Bill's intent to ensure efficient spending. The reality is that councils don't control the levers for many of the key funding and financing tools that could be used to minimize waste.

Councils and communities have steadily advocated for the use of packages of tools by government and businesses to enable effective and efficient waste minimization and management. Central government has the power to create a strong, stable regulatory framework and secure funding models waste minimization. But we've seen successive governments choosing not to do so over the last 20 years.

This is Lee of councils and communities picking up the pieces and it's given companies the freedom to put packaging and products onto the market that impose a wide variety of costs on communities and society. So we think that waste minimization needs to be a core activity for councils. We recommend that this committee looks really carefully at the, at the definitions because we see a lot of problems with the way these things are been defined and then the relationships out into other acts.

And also, um, we really wanna see the wording clear and consistent, but we do wanna see waste minimization given the priority it deserves. We know that that's what New Zealanders support because survey after survey shows that they're really keen to see better, um, ways to prevent and reduce waste, um, have access to the services that enable small business, um, visitors and households and resident residents to be able to do that.

So yeah, happy to take any questions that you guys might have. Two minutes for questions. Anyone? Alright. Yes, please. Your waters. There you go. Um, thank you very much for your submission, which I found really fascinating. I was just curious about, um, council behavior after council's behavior after the 2007 auditor general report that you mentioned.

Was that a bit of a turning point? And we have, we seen radical shift in terms of, um, the capacity going into waste management and, uh, facilities versus services. So what does that Ratio kind of look like? Well, there has been quite a big investment over the last 15 or 20 years by councils in, um, things like recycling, composting behavior change and education programs, supporting things like repair cafes and other, um, ways of getting people to think differently about the products that they handle.

But I think the main thing I would say is that that report really made it very clear to everybody that councils don't control the levers. You know, they're not the ones who can really say, okay, if you're putting this product onto the market, then you need to cover the real cost of recycling it across its life. Um, and we, you know, it's very clear that, um, central government hasn't stepped up.

They haven't put in place product stewardship schemes. They haven't, you know, we've seen this government backing down on quite a few of the things that they said they, that would be progressed, you know, over the, through the, the, the things that were work in progress. So what I would say is councils are doing their best. They don't control the levers, the, you know, they're getting squeezed from both directions, whether to reduce spending, but also at the same time they've got a really high, um, you know, expectation coming onto them from central

government in terms of an unfunded mandate to reduce and prevent waste. And then from their communities to provide them that serve with services that actually makes sense and are doing a good job. And the cost of that is going up and up. There's a good report by Wasteman that looks at, you know, the, the, the steadily increasing cost to councils of their waste budgets.

And I guess the problem that I see with this situation is that we could end up, um, you know, with a mandate to continue to spend on rubbish disposal, but no, no potential for councils to actually spend on things which would see them reduce costs and risk over the time. Thank you very much for that. We've actually run out of time. We've got, thank you for joining us Today. Thanks so much. Yeah.
Really appreciate it. Thanks Luke. See you next. We have environment. Southland.

Are we good? Welcome. You have 10 minutes with us. Kira. I'm Nick Niel Hore, chairman of Environment Southland. I'm joined by Hay Fit General Manager of Strategy and Regulation. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. On behalf of Environment Southland about the local government systems improvement bill, we broadly support the Bills goals of improving transparency and efficiency, but we have serious concerns about several of the proposed changes and their unintended consequences for regional councils like ourselves.

Firstly, we strongly opposed the proposed change to Section 10, which removes the four wellbeings while elevating economic growth and development above other vital functions. Such an approach risks deprioritizing regional council's, critical statutory responsibilities including flood management, catchment and waterway protection, environmental regulation, and biosecurity with a Iraq cap being muted, the reprioritization of these functions could undermine the effectiveness of these critical safeguards, increasing the risk

in the national EN environment. This in turn could have severe repercussions for the South Southland's economy, which relies heavily on the health of its ecosystems to protect lives, properties, and the economy. In Southland, we manage substantial flood resilience assets worth 460 million in total, including a drainage network of almost 1400 kilometers.

With climate change and an increasing flood risk, it will be deeply concerning if these vital services were compromised due to new funding or legislative restraints. Straints additionally, removing the cult cultural wellbeing mandate risks, undermining the role of Man Fe, particularly at nhu, our enduring and constructive partnership and managing resource management issues.

We believe these values and worldviews are essential in achieving long-term sustainable outcomes for the South end community, including the sustained growth of the south end economy. N Tahu plays a integral role in many industries, including farming and fisheries.

The commercial aspirations by ensuring the resource management system aligns well with iwi values and operates within harmony with, with their drug area. A strong preference is to delete clause six of the bill with consequence amendments to other clauses as required. Haley.

In terms of, um, core services, the proposed definition of core services is too narrow and we are concerned it will force councils to divert funding away from equally important, but apparently non-core statutory functions. For instance, biosecurity in Southland is at risk.

Take the case of Aria in Fjord land after 15 years and millions invested, we have successfully eradicated it from some areas, but funding limits will likely make this more challenging. In future, if biosecurity isn't recognized as a core service of local government, critical environmental values could be lost with a flow on severe consequence for our economy.

Our recommendation is to remove the core services concept entirely by deleting clause seven and 18 of the bill. We support moves towards greater transparency and accountability such as the proposed standardized codes of conduct and standing orders.

However, councils must retain the ability to adapt these documents to reflect local context. A rigid one size fits all approach risks overlooking the diverse needs of our communities and may limit council's ability to respond effectively to local priorities. Similarly, we strongly advise caution about proposals around benchmarking and the regulation of council activities.

Councils operate in vastly different social economic and geographic contexts, no more so than Southland comparisons without nuance risk creating false equivalencies that could undermine public trust or stifle local innovation. Furthermore, the culture of competition could reduce cooperation and breed division potentially hindering established shared services that have consistently achieved cost efficiencies and delivered strong outcomes, especially in critical sectors such as Transport Council is also concerned that specifying the scope

of activities that can be addressed in long-term plans, annual plans and annual reports and regulations, could significantly compromise local democracy as democratically elected bodies representing local residents. And with a thorough understanding of our region, councils should have the discretion to prioritize goals and projects according to the specific needs of their community and ensuring the best possible and most cost efficient outcomes.

So to address these concerns, we recommend deleting sub clauses 21 and Bracket two to remove the requirements for benchmarking and the identification of core services. We support modernizing public notice requirements as current rules requiring newspaper advertising are outdated and expensive for our rate payers.

However, we strongly oppose the proposal to remove the requirement for councils to consider whether Tik Maori acknowledge Tik Maori knowledge may be relevant to the governance of a council controlled organization. This requirement presents a valuable opportunity to integrate Tik Maori into public services with minimal financial investment.

As for six yearly service delivery reviews, we strongly believe that these are still valuable for us. They give elected members visibility and insight into how well our services are being delivered. Therefore, we recommend deleting clause 16 and amending clause nine to ensure councils continue to review the cost effectiveness of service delivery.

In closing and in summary, environment, Southland supports the Bill's intent, but key amendments are needed to ensure that environmental and biosecurity functions aren't deprioritized, particularly given their close connection to economic productivity and economic growth.

Partnerships are protected and enhanced councils retain the flexibility and autonomy needed to serve diverse communities and that the cost pressures from new regulations are fairly considered. And at Environment Southland, we are absolutely committed to delivering good governance and public value, but that requires legislation that reflects the full scope of our responsibilities, not just the economic ones.

Nui, thank you so much for the opportunity and we are very happy to answer any questions that you may have of us. Uh, thank you for your submissions. I go to Als. Uh, thank you very much for your submission and, and thank you also for the alternative proposals that you put on page three. I thought that was extremely helpful and practical.

Um, I had a question on your paragraph 12 where you talk about, um, natural hazard and mitigation, void avoidance being replaced by civil defense emergency management. Um, I recently had a conversation with the disability commissioner at the human rights commissioner get to the question, the course that there are obligations in, in terms of reasonable accommodation.

Um, so my question really is how you view that, um, and whether you would view that as preventing you do doing that work for the disabled community. Thank you so much for the question. That would be a concern that we would similarly share. Um, we feel that the framing of natural hazard mitigation and avoidance is so narrow that it actually leaves quite a lot in question around what the government's expectation is of us with respect to our, um, broader functions beyond just civil defense emergency management, what that specifically means,

but as its degree related into more general functions around flood hazards, um, we have, we are, we are a pretty hazardous region and how we might serve those communities, particularly those with very specific needs through that function. And so we, we don't want there to be, um, to be doubt, but we, we do want us there to be scope to serve our communities well.

50 seconds will last. Uh, okay. Um, you've asked about statutory functions, um, and um, a lot of other people have asked about that as well. If we just put statutory functions into the core, uh, definition of core, would that help? It probably, it probably would help. And I guess we we're happy with national direction, but all the regions are different.

And so having regional flag I think's important. I mean the, the suggestion of, um, um, benchmarking and capping, we've got quite a bit of flood, um, where it needs to be done. We have to provide 40% of it would be a tragedy if we could not, um, actually improve our flood resilience because of budget.

Cons, constraints. And look, if I can secondly the, um, just you, you basically said scrap all the, all the, um, bit of a measuring and benchmarking, et cetera. How do we get a reasonable comparison if we don't have something there to get a reasonable comparison? Take all the points about nuances and every council is different, um, between councils.

So actually transparently these councils are doing well. These ones maybe not doing quite as well, need to improve performance. I think, you know, the regional sector, we, we are pretty collaborative and we could come up with something that that could be measurable. Obviously with size and different functions it won't be perfect, but, uh, but I think people want to see that compared with apples.

With apples. Um, no, no problem with that. But, um, it, it is probably difficult to get a simple thing that is, that's effective. Um, we've seen it with taxpayers union and it's not totally accurate for regional councils, but we're, we're not, not opposed to that. Transparency's paramount. Yeah, we absolutely welcome the opportunity to work, um, with, uh, government on what that looks like.

We have had some really productive conversations with the Department of Internal Affairs on the recently released benchmarking that the government did. And I think that that was a really elegant explanation of how it's different horses for courses sometimes and that we, we are a fairly different region to most. I appreciate that. Thank you. Thank you very Much. We've gone well over time. Have a good day. Thank you.
